"Yet this night [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] sinned, daring to undo my work in [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] father’s house. Do not mistake [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] will for a living sacrifice." A cruel smile twisted the stone lips. "Come, then, cast thyself down and follow me, embrace my bounty, and I shall spare [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] the inevitable. I shall offer unto [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] true might and protection, and rest from the repetitious agony of straight and narrow paths."
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1448:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/(X)_Chapter_1__Stardust_to_Stardust_(1).txt:45:"Look about [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE], boy," Dagon whispered. "Notice the battle within and the wars between. Death is not a distant shore. It is the very sea wherein [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] swimmest. I offer unto [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] a raft, a haven amidst the tempest. Choose me, and I shall shield [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] from its sting. I shall bestow upon [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] power over the elements themselves."
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1449:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/(X)_Chapter_1__Stardust_to_Stardust_(1).txt:47:"I’d rather drown standing than float in your shadow!" Dan interrupted with a snarl, a fierce, primal refusal.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1450:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/(X)_Chapter_1__Stardust_to_Stardust_(1).txt:48:A flicker of profound, ancient disappointment crossed Dagon’s vast face, quickly replaced by a predatory hunger. "You might live. But those whom [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] holdest dear shall live too, and in their living find only hardship. Their wells will run dry, their prayers shall be as a dry echo against a silent sky. Defy me, and they will live their lives as dust in a barren land. And they will despise the light [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] seekest, for it offers them no comfort, only a cold, indifferent judgment. And [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] defiance shall be met with the perfect, terrible silence of a curse." This was the promise of forsaken.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1451:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/(X)_Chapter_1__Stardust_to_Stardust_(1).txt:50:"If ever [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] shouldst alter [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] score," Dagon hissed a fermata in cantabile as the nightmare diminuendoed, "then merely knock thrice, ask twice, then wait. I shall come, for I am the cadence you seek to outrun".
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1452:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:3:The torch's frail flame threw suspicious shadows across the narrow hall, trembling like the boy's own heart—a muffled drumbeat trapped against his ribs. Each barefoot step on the cold, sun-baked stone was a gamble, the slightest shift threatening a clank or a dry scrape that could betray his silent, desperate mission. The silence was not the peace of a resting world; it was a vast, coiled emptiness, the patient dread of a serpent in a garden waiting for the inevitable misstep. This was not a home, but the heavy, unmoving lair of one that had deliberately chosen the low places, devoted to the sheer, magnetic pull of the earth's downward weight.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1453:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:7:Piles of broken pottery shards loomed like ancient, crumbling cairns, their stained edges catching the light, whispering not of forgotten meals, but of the sharp, cutting weight of history. Rotting textiles, already teeming with slow, white life, draped over discarded, brittle animal bones, making a faint, disturbing whisper—the dry rustle of a life consumed. Withered sandals and rusted bronze tools lay scattered like the forgotten toys of a peevish god. Desiccated fruits, once bursting with the radiant promise of life, now clung to their seeds like mummified burdens, a stark testament to a cursed harvest. The air itself was a torment—a vile, cloying stench of desiccated herbs, stagnant decay, and human waste, sickly sweet, a perfume that spoke of a final, total surrender.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1454:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:9:The boy, whose messy hair and kind eyes had been forced to witness too much nakedness of the soul, picked his way through the refuse. A profound, bone-deep chill settled on his spine, born not just of the filth, but from the palpable wrongness—a tangible sense of a man who had glimpsed the highest peak and deliberately, consciously chosen the shadow. His father, Aviel, once a scholar whose wisdom was sought by countless seekers, now saw treasure in rot, abundance in decay, and comfort in pity. He had traded insight for squalor, a mind once a bright flame now choked and clouded by the accumulating debris of his own choices. This divided house was slowly consuming them, a willing living sacrifice to the dark comfort of the low places.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1455:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:11:A heavy, psychic weight had settled on Dan's spirit, and he yearned to cast it off like a soiled garment. For seven years, he had watched helplessly as his father lost himself. The house had slowly become a tomb of waste, each passing season adding another layer of debris, another shadow to Aviel’s eyes. The decision to leave this infected home, to break the blood-bond with corruption, was perhaps the hardest he had ever faced—a tearing at the very roots of his being, a violent spiritual separation. He had to leave this Sodom before he, too, was consumed. Looking back was not an option.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1456:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:15:As he reached his father’s doorframe, he held his breath, and with a quiet snuff, deliberately extinguished the torch. He plunged the immediate area into a deeper, more absolute darkness, relying now only on the faint, alien moonlight filtering through the narrow slit in the hallway wall and the sharp memory of light. The pungent smell of burnt wick lingered, a bitter tang of parting in the stagnant air. He confirmed his father was asleep; deep sleep filled the room. The ache in Dan's chest was not new, but it had grown heavier—a cold, dull throb behind his ribs, the physical cost for choosing the upward path.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1457:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:25:The journey began not with a lift, but a climb. He found himself at the base of a staircase of pure thought, a translucent helix of solidified starlight winding into the dark, star-shot void—a monument to the verticality of the soul. He placed his bare foot on the first riser, an act of pure will-to-overcome, his gaze locked on the single, impossibly distant star that was his goal. The helix morphed into a single, thick rope, heavy and vibrating with the predatory energy of the low places it pierced. This rope was both his lifeline and his anchor, threatening to pull him down with a relentless, sickening gravity the moment the slightest sliver of comfort entered his heart. The intense pressure of the ascent, the pain of leaving everything familiar behind, was a raw, sharp agony in the marrow of his chest—the cost of the living sacrifice.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1458:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:27:He climbed until the very air around him was thin and cold, ringing with the silence of the unmanifest. He forced the last dreck of earthly attachment from his mind—a memory of his father's clean face, a phantom scent of Zuna's hair, the dull weight of the silver he still carried in his heart. The rope snapped. He fell, a terrifying, soul-wrenching drop through the shattered dreamscape, plunging not into darkness, but into a stagnant, empty field illuminated by a shy, ethereal gray light. He had landed, with the soundless impact of a stone dropped into velvet, deep within his father’s sleeping mind. It was a formless void teeming with potential. As things were before Time when the first words of Truth were spoken.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1459:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:29:As Aviel stood alone in the very center, a solitary figure waiting to dream, dust began to swirl around him, the raw, indistinguishable material of creation itself, the dust from which all things arise. Dan drew a breath, not of air, but of pure, sovereign will, and with it, a primal surge of power flowed through him. The dust, stirred by his absolute intention, began to coil into a swirling vortex of transformation. The unmanifest thickened and darkened. Then Dan exhaled, a soundless act of command, and whispered three words into the silence:
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1460:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:33:First, glittering, countless motes of sand rained down, then knit together, drawing close, hardening, forming smooth, cool pebbles and then heavy stones that clicked softly as they accumulated. The unnatural, accelerated genesis of the stones was a clear, devastating image—sharp, cutting, accumulating weight. Aviel, lost in the deep currents of his own self-delusion, and prodded by Dan's word and will, perceived only treasure. He scrabbled at the growing piles, his fingers frantic, his hands shaking with a desperate greed, stuffing his pockets and then his garments.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1461:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:39:Drained but with a renewed sense of purpose, Dan gingerly left his father's room, the darkness of the hallway a sudden, heavy, oppressive presence. He felt smaller now, his borrowed confidence spent, leaving him vulnerable once more to the oppressive reality of the house. Each shadow seemed to watch as he carefully navigated the maze on the now freezing stone floor, tiptoeing back toward the hearth. He knelt by the glowing coals, extended a steady hand, then he used a small piece of kindling to gently coax a tiny flame from the embers. The flame caught with a quiet hiss. He lit his torch, its flame now a familiar, hopeful presence in the gloom, and with new, resolute purpose, turned and made his way back through the refuse.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1462:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:43:He found himself, at first, standing on a shore of glittering, black sand. The water was unnaturally calm, stretching out like a sheet of polished glass to meet a horizon that glowed with a faint, hopeful blue. A sense of profound, unburdened peace washed over him, a terrifying softness. He saw a small, sturdy boat floating nearby, its single sail full of an unseen, gentle breeze. He forced the boat away. He stepped out onto the water, and it held him. He was halfway across the cove when the glass-like water beneath his feet shattered with a deafening crack, pulling the false foundation out from under him. The peaceful blue on the horizon bled instantly into an angry, bruised purple. A cold, sudden gust of wind screamed past his ears. The dream was no longer his.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1463:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:49:In the far north, where the sea turned to ink, a monstrous tail breached the surface, a colossal, serpentine fin that dwarfed his shattered plank. Scaled and shining, it lashed back and forth. The body, vast, unknowable, and stretching beneath the waves, remained submerged, an unseen horror connecting the humanoid head and the serpentine tail fin into a single, terrifying entity whose being was the essence of material power.
./reports/ingestion-hash-references.txt:1464:src/data-layer/ingestion-buffer/gdrive_raw/*Chapter_1:_Stardust_to_Stardust.txt:57:"[PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] ever called, and ever dost call. [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] people did so. [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] very flesh [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] so," Dagon rumbled. "Yet this night [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] sinned, daring to undo my work in [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] father’s house. Do not mistake [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] will for a living sacrifice." A cruel smile twisted the stone lips. "Come, then, cast thyself down and follow me, embrace my bounty, and I shall spare [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] the inevitable. I shall offer unto [PRIMORDIAL_SEVERE_MANDATE] true might and protection, and rest from the repetitious agony of straight and narrow paths."
